title: Read the Receipt, Not the Tea Leaves
context: A receipt-capture architecture review has been scheduled after a polished vendor demo impressed the leadership team. The Product Director wants a smooth mobile experience for a customer showcase. The Tax Lead cares about evidence quality. Security wants durable controls around stored images, and Finance is already asking whether OCR can also help with duplicate and fraud detection. You are the architect expected to keep the room focused.

questions.prompt: The vendor says: "If you adopt our suite, you get OCR, tax extraction, duplicate detection, and anomaly scoring in one purchase." The Product Director looks interested. How do you respond?

options.text: That sounds efficient, so we should take the full suite while the receipt work is active. Buying one package now avoids later integration work and gives us a stronger conference story than a narrower capture-only decision.
options.explanation: The desire to consolidate vendors is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts a bundled heavyweight option before separating capture, evidence, and downstream control problems.

options.text: I would separate receipt capture from the broader control stack before we commit. If the immediate requirement is reliable image capture and field extraction, we should evaluate that on its own merits and only add duplicate or anomaly capabilities when we can justify them with audit, latency, and ownership criteria.
options.explanation: This demonstrates naming the domain and applying criteria before selecting a heavier platform. The answer stops a bundle from becoming the architecture by default and keeps the room on the requirement that is live today.

options.text: We should not buy any vendor capability here because building everything ourselves keeps the architecture cleaner. External suites always introduce dependency risk, so the only safe route is a fully in-house service.
options.explanation: The concern about dependency risk is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it rejects the proposal categorically instead of framing which capability is commodity and which might deserve internal ownership.

questions.prompt: Security asks: "If OCR extracts the fields we need, can we discard the image and store only structured data?" What do you say?

options.text: Yes, once the text is extracted the image becomes redundant. Keeping fewer files lowers storage cost and narrows the security surface, while the structured data gives Product and Finance everything they need downstream.
options.explanation: The storage concern is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it treats extracted fields as a full substitute for the evidence record without testing audit and dispute requirements.

options.text: I would keep the immutable image as evidence and store extracted fields alongside it for workflow and reporting use. They solve different problems: one preserves what was submitted, and the other supports operational processing. Discarding the source weakens audit and review when OCR confidence is imperfect.
options.explanation: This demonstrates separating evidence from operational data and anchoring on audit. The answer makes the trust boundary explicit instead of collapsing two distinct needs into one record.

options.text: Let us ask the vendor what other customers do before we decide. If their common pattern is to keep only structured fields, we can follow that practice and avoid overthinking it.
options.explanation: The wish to learn from peers is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already frame the decision by distinguishing evidence preservation from operational convenience without outsourcing the thinking.

questions.prompt: The Tax Lead says: "OCR confidence will vary by country and receipt style. Should we run a second OCR service in parallel so we can cross-check every submission?" How do you respond?

options.text: Yes, dual-running two OCR engines from day one gives us the strongest answer. It reduces the chance of missed fields, produces richer data, and lets us compare vendors continuously while the product scales.
options.explanation: The quality concern is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it jumps to a materially heavier operating model before the room has defined acceptable confidence levels or review behaviour.

options.text: I would translate that into a confidence and review requirement first. If the issue is unreliable extraction on low-confidence receipts, a review queue with clear thresholds may solve it without paying for two live OCR paths. We should only dual-run vendors if the confidence target or regulatory risk truly rules the simpler path out.
options.explanation: This demonstrates translating a concern into a requirement and naming the simpler option. The answer keeps the room on thresholds, review, and risk rather than assuming parallel services are automatically the rigorous choice.

options.text: OCR is always imperfect, so I would avoid promising any structured extraction at all. Employees can upload the image, and finance teams can key the fields manually until we revisit automation later.
options.explanation: The caution about confidence is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it responds to uncertainty by collapsing the feature instead of offering a bounded, auditable workflow.

questions.prompt: Finance adds: "While we are doing receipt OCR, we should also include duplicate detection and spend anomaly scoring in the same release." What is your response?

options.text: That is sensible because the image and extracted data are already flowing through the pipeline. Adding the extra controls now would make the release feel more strategic and avoid another architecture cycle later.
options.explanation: The desire to build on existing work is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it accepts a classic 'while we are doing this' move and couples receipt capture to a broader control agenda without separating the decisions.

options.text: I would keep receipt capture as the decision in this meeting and treat duplicate or anomaly controls as separate follow-on problems. We can design the capture events so they do not block future controls, but that is different from committing this release to a broader fraud platform scope.
options.explanation: This demonstrates resisting scope creep while still leaving a defensible future path. The answer keeps the current decision bounded and prevents an adjacent capability from hijacking the release.

options.text: We need a larger fraud workshop before we can answer. Once Finance, Risk, and Product align on every control they may want over the next year, we can revisit the receipt design with the full roadmap in view.
options.explanation: The intent to coordinate is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already protect scope and still leave room for later control design without pausing the current decision.

questions.prompt: The room asks you to close. Which summary best reflects the meeting?

options.text: We will evaluate OCR as a bounded capture-and-extraction problem, retain receipt images as evidence alongside structured fields, use confidence thresholds and review before introducing heavier dual-vendor operation, and keep duplicate or anomaly controls out of this release unless a separate requirement justifies them. That gives Product and Security a clear design boundary to work within.
options.explanation: This demonstrates strong meeting closure. It confirms the bounded scope, the criteria used, and the immediate consequences for delivery without reopening adjacent debates.

options.text: The prudent next step is to wait for more vendor responses and gather additional international receipt samples before deciding anything. That will give us a stronger basis for a more complete architecture recommendation next month.
options.explanation: The caution is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the room already has enough to decide the design principles even if some vendor selection detail remains open.

options.text: We should pursue the broadest possible receipt platform now so capture, fraud checks, duplicate detection, and analytics all land as one coherent initiative. That keeps the architecture ambitious and avoids redesign later.
options.explanation: The ambition is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it treats every adjacent capability as part of the same decision and turns a bounded capture problem into a much larger platform commitment.
